moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, seizing central space and opening diagonals. This is the move that allows the Alekhine Defense to exist — Black's provocative reply directly targets this pawn.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn in the center. White expects to maintain or expand this center.
plans: Build a strong center and develop pieces behind it
Respond to Black's provocations by advancing the center pawns

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Alekhine Defense — one of the most provocative openings in chess. Black develops a knight to ATTACK e4 on move one, daring White to push e5 and chase it. The whole concept is hypermodern: let White advance their pawns, then undermine the overextended center. It's like a matador waving a red cape at the bull.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn commitment from Black. The idea is to let White advance and then attack the resulting pawn chain.
plans: Provoke e5, then maneuver the knight to continue provoking advances
Undermine White's overextended center with ...d6, ...c5, or ...f6
alternatives: 1...e5 — classical and solid, doesn't provoke White's center
1...c5 (Sicilian) — fights for the center differently, without knight provocation

moveAnnotations.annotation: White accepts the challenge and pushes e5, gaining space and kicking the knight. This is the most natural response — why wouldn't you push a pawn with tempo? White gains central territory, but the e5 pawn is also a potential target. The danger for White is that each tempo spent chasing the knight is a tempo not spent on development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The e5 pawn gains space but is advanced and potentially vulnerable. White must be careful not to overextend.
plans: Continue advancing to build a massive center with d4, c4
Develop pieces quickly to support the advanced center
alternatives: 2.Nc3 — the Vienna-like approach, solid but lets Black equalize easily

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight retreats to d5, a powerful central square. From d5, it's stable and well-placed — it controls b4, c3, e3, and f4. Black hasn't wasted a move; the knight is actually better on d5 than f6 in many positions. And the e5 pawn? It's now a target that Black will undermine with ...d6.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn change yet. Black's strategy depends on attacking White's center with ...d6 soon.
plans: Play ...d6 to challenge the e5 pawn
If White plays c4, retreat to b6 and continue the provocation with ...d6
alternatives: 2...Ng8 — the Brooklyn Defense, retreating all the way back; too passive

moveAnnotations.annotation: White builds the two-pawn center with d4, supporting e5 and claiming dominant central space. This is the standard approach — d4 and e5 together control all the key squares. The center looks imposing, but Black's entire opening philosophy is built around attacking exactly this kind of structure.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The d4-e5 pawn duo gives White a significant space advantage. Black must challenge this with ...d6 to avoid being squeezed.
plans: Support the center with c4 to kick the knight and maintain space
Develop pieces behind the strong center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at the base of White's center with ...d6. This is the critical moment in the Alekhine — Black directly challenges the e5 pawn. If White captures exd6, the center dissolves and Black equalizes easily. If White maintains the pawn on e5, Black will continue pressuring it. The whole opening depends on whether White's center is a strength or a liability.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After ...d6, the central tension is at its peak. The resolution (exd6, or maintaining e5) will determine the entire middlegame character.
plans: Challenge and undermine White's center
Develop pieces to pressure e5 and d4
alternatives: 3...d6 could also be played on move 2, but Nd5 first is more flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, supporting both d4 and e5 while preparing to castle. This is the Modern Variation — White prioritizes development over the aggressive c4 chase. The knight on f3 is rock-solid: it defends the center, helps with kingside development, and doesn't overextend.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White maintains the d4-e5 center. The position is less aggressive than the Four Pawns Attack but more stable.
plans: Castle kingside quickly and maintain a solid center
Play Be2 and develop harmoniously
alternatives: 4.c4 (Four Pawns Attack) — the most aggressive, chasing the knight with c4-c5 but risking overextension
4.Bc4 — targets the knight on d5 directly

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black pins the f3 knight to the queen — a very logical developing move. The bishop on g4 creates real pressure: if White plays Be2, Black can trade bishops and double White's pawns. The pin also makes it harder for White to play f4, which would otherwise support the e5 pawn. This is Black at their most active in the Alekhine.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change, but the pin on f3 limits White's ability to reinforce the center.
plans: Maintain the pin and develop the rest of the pieces
If White plays Be2, consider ...Bxe2 Qxe2 to damage White's structure
alternatives: 4...Bf5 — develops the bishop but doesn't create the pin pressure

moveAnnotations.annotation: White breaks the pin by developing the bishop to e2. This is the calm, positional approach — the bishop on e2 is modest but solid, it unpins the knight and prepares castling. White is saying: 'I'll keep my strong center and develop normally.' It's less ambitious than h3 (which asks the bishop to declare itself) but very reliable.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. The center remains intact and White is ready to castle.
plans: Castle kingside and complete development
Play c4 later to expand on the queenside
alternatives: 5.h3 — forces the bishop to choose, but weakens the kingside slightly

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays ...e6, a subtle but important move. It reinforces the knight on d5, prepares ...Be7 for development, and keeps the position solid. The e6 pawn also supports a potential ...f6 break later to undermine e5. Black is building a compact, resilient structure rather than seeking immediate action.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid e6-d6 chain. The light-squared bishop will need to come back inside the chain, but it's already done its job on g4.
plans: Develop ...Be7 and castle kingside
Prepare ...f6 or ...c5 to challenge the center
alternatives: 5...dxe5 — opens the center but gives White free development with dxe5

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles, getting the king to safety and connecting the rooks. With a strong center and completed kingside development, White has a very comfortable position. The castled king also means the rook on f1 can support an f4 push if needed, adding another defender to the e5 pawn.
plans: Play c4 to expand and kick the d5 knight
Use the rook on f1 to support f4 if needed

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a modest but necessary square. The bishop prepares castling and covers some important dark squares. In the Alekhine, Black's pieces are often placed on conservative squares — the compensation comes from the structural pressure on White's center, not from flashy piece play.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. Black's setup with e6-d6 and Be7 is solid and prepares to castle.
plans: Castle kingside immediately
Prepare ...c5 or ...f6 to challenge the center
alternatives: 6...Bd6 — more ambitious, but the bishop can be hit by c4 and Nc3

moveAnnotations.annotation: White strikes at the powerful d5 knight, forcing it to retreat. The c4 push also grabs more space on the queenside and supports d5 if White ever pushes there. This is the moment of truth for the knight — it must leave its comfortable central outpost and find a new home.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White now has pawns on c4, d4, and e5 — a massive pawn center. But each advance also makes the structure slightly harder to maintain.
plans: Drive the knight away and maintain the space advantage
Develop Nc3 and control the center completely

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight retreats to b6, the standard square in this line. From b6, the knight eyes c4 and d5, staying relevant despite being pushed back. It's not as dominant as d5, but it's well-placed — it pressures c4 and can reroute via c8-d6 if needed. The knight has done its job: White's center is big but potentially overextended.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change. The knight on b6 puts pressure on c4 and d5.
plans: Castle and finish development
Play ...Bxf3 at the right moment to double White's pawns
Prepare ...c5 or ...f6 to undermine the center
alternatives: 7...Nb4 — aggressive but the knight gets hit by a3

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to c3, completing minor piece development. The knight protects e4 (if the pawn were still there) and controls d5 — a vital outpost in this pawn structure. With Nc3, White's development is essentially complete: all minor pieces are out, the king is safe, and the center is strong. It's a textbook position.
plans: Build on the space advantage with Be3, Rc1
Play for a d5 or f4 advance in the middlegame

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles, finally getting the king to safety. Both sides are now fully developed, and the real battle begins. Black's position is compact and solid — the e6-d6 chain is resilient, the knight on b6 has purpose, and the bishop pair is a long-term asset. White has more space, but Black has no weaknesses. This is a typical Alekhine middlegame: White tries to convert space into an attack, Black tries to undermine the center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The position is stable: White has pawns on c4-d4-e5, Black has e6-d6. Black will target c4 and e5, White will try to advance or maintain the center.
plans: Play ...Bxf3 to double White's pawns and weaken the center
Prepare ...c5 or ...f6 to break down the pawn chain

moveAnnotations.annotation: White stakes a claim to the center, controlling the d5 and f5 squares while opening lines for the bishop and queen. This move prepares rapid development and gives White early space advantage, setting the stage for an aggressive pawn storm in the Four Pawns Attack.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Classical central pawn on e4, controlling key central squares and providing a foundation for White's attacking ambitions.
plans: Advance with e5 to cramp Black's position
Develop pieces harmoniously behind the pawn center

moveAnnotations.annotation: The signature move of Alekhine's Defence! Black immediately attacks the e4 pawn, forcing White to make an immediate decision. This hypermodern approach invites White to advance pawns while Black prepares to undermine the extended pawn chain later. It's a provocative opening that leads to sharp, tactical positions.
plans: Retreat the knight when attacked, then strike at White's pawn center
Develop pieces to pressure White's advanced pawns
alternatives: 1...e5 leads to standard King's Pawn openings

moveAnnotations.annotation: White accepts Black's challenge and advances, gaining space and forcing the knight to retreat. This move controls f6 and d6, cramping Black's position in the short term. However, this advanced pawn becomes a potential target and White must be prepared to support it with more pawns and pieces.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates a space advantage with the advanced e5 pawn, but this pawn chain will become extended and potentially vulnerable as more pawns advance.
plans: Support the e5 pawn with d4
Continue advancing pawns to maintain space advantage

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight retreats to its ideal square, where it's perfectly centralized and eyes multiple squares. From d5, the knight controls key central squares and can't be easily dislodged by pawns. This move also prepares to challenge White's pawn center from a strong outpost, embodying the hypermodern strategy of controlling the center with pieces rather than pawns.
plans: Use the centralized knight to pressure White's position
Prepare ...d6 to challenge the e5 pawn

moveAnnotations.annotation: White continues the pawn storm, supporting the e5 pawn and controlling more central territory. This move prevents ...Nc3+ and prepares the advance c4, which will attack the knight on d5. The pawn chain e5-d4 looks imposing but requires constant support and creates potential weaknesses on the kingside light squares.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White builds a classical pawn center with pawns on e5 and d4, controlling key central squares but committing to an aggressive, space-consuming strategy.
plans: Play c4 to attack the centralized knight
Continue building the pawn chain with f4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at the foundation of White's pawn chain! This move challenges the e5 pawn directly, creating immediate tension in the center. By attacking the advanced pawn, Black begins the counterplay that's essential to the Alekhine Defense, forcing White to either trade pawns or defend the extended pawn structure.
plans: Continue pressuring the e5 pawn
Prepare piece development to increase pressure on White's center
alternatives: 6...c6 prepares ...Nb6 but doesn't challenge the center immediately

moveAnnotations.annotation: The third pawn in White's aggressive advance! This move attacks the centralized knight on d5, forcing it to move again while gaining more space. White is now committed to the Four Pawns Attack strategy - a double-edged approach that gives maximum space but creates potential weaknesses. The pawns look intimidating but become targets for Black's pieces.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's pawn chain now stretches from c4 to e5, controlling enormous space but becoming increasingly difficult to defend as it extends further from White's pieces.
plans: Force the knight away with the pawn attack
Complete the four-pawn formation with f4

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight retreats but stays active, eyeing the c4 and d5 squares while preparing to support the coming ...dxe5 break. From b6, the knight can later jump to d7 or a4, and it's perfectly placed to support Black's counterplay against White's extended pawn chain. This move also clears the d5 square for potential pawn breaks.
plans: Support the central pawn break with ...dxe5
Consider ...Na4 to attack the bishop when it develops

moveAnnotations.annotation: The fourth and final pawn in White's aggressive formation! White completes the intimidating pawn wall from c4 to f4, controlling maximum space and preparing kingside attacking chances. However, this extension also creates serious weaknesses - the kingside is opened, the king's safety is compromised, and the advanced pawns become juicy targets for Black's pieces.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The complete Four Pawns Attack formation: c4-d4-e5-f4. This gives White enormous space but creates multiple weaknesses, especially on the kingside light squares and around White's king position.
plans: Support the pawn chain with piece development
Prepare for kingside attacking chances
alternatives: 9.Nf3 develops more safely but is less aggressive

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at the heart of White's position! This pawn break eliminates White's most advanced pawn and opens the center, where Black's pieces will be more active. The move also opens the d-file for Black's heavy pieces and creates immediate tactical complications. This is the key counterplay move that justifies Black's opening setup.
plans: Develop pieces actively to exploit the opened center
Use the d-file for rook activity

moveAnnotations.annotation: White recaptures, maintaining the pawn on e5 but now the f-file is half-open and the king's position is more exposed. This recapture keeps material equal but gives Black excellent piece activity in return. The pawn structure has shifted, and White must now deal with Black's active piece play while trying to consolidate the position.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White maintains the e5 pawn but the pawn chain is now c4-d4-e5, and the f-file is half-open, potentially dangerous for White's king safety.
plans: Develop pieces to support the center
Castle to safety as quickly as possible

moveAnnotations.annotation: Excellent piece development! The knight jumps to its most active square, attacking the d4 pawn and preparing to coordinate with other pieces. From c6, the knight supports central control and puts immediate pressure on White's pawn center. This move also prepares castling and brings Black closer to completing development with a harmonious piece setup.
plans: Continue pressuring the d4 pawn
Complete development with ...Bf5 and prepare castling

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to defend the d4 pawn while preparing to castle queenside. This move supports the center and connects the rooks, but the bishop on e3 can become a target for Black's pieces later. The bishop also eyes the kingside, particularly the h6 square, supporting potential attacking ideas.
plans: Castle queenside to safety
Support the central pawn structure

moveAnnotations.annotation: A perfect developing move that puts the bishop on an aggressive square! The bishop on f5 eyes White's kingside, particularly the vulnerable e4 and h3 squares, while also supporting Black's central control. This move completes Black's piece activity and puts maximum pressure on White's somewhat loose position. The bishop is particularly well-placed to exploit the weaknesses created by White's pawn advances.
plans: Continue active piece development
Look for tactical opportunities against White's exposed king

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to its natural square, supporting the center and preparing further development. The knight on c3 defends the d4 pawn and opens up possibilities for queenside castling. However, this move also means White's king is still in the center while Black's pieces are becoming increasingly active and coordinated.
plans: Complete development and castle queenside
Support the central pawn structure

moveAnnotations.annotation: A subtle but strong move that prepares ...Be7 and castling while also restraining White's advanced pawns. This pawn move controls the d5 and f5 squares, preventing White from advancing further, and opens up the diagonal for Black's light-squared bishop. The move also solidifies Black's position and prepares to complete development safely.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid pawn structure that restrains White's pawns while preparing harmonious piece development.
plans: Develop the bishop to e7 and castle kingside
Complete piece coordination
alternatives: 15...Qd7 develops the queen immediately but doesn't prepare castling

moveAnnotations.annotation: White finally develops the kingside knight, supporting the center and preparing to castle. The knight on f3 defends key squares and supports the e5 pawn, but White is falling behind in development and king safety. Black's pieces are more active and better coordinated, giving Black excellent practical chances despite White's space advantage.
plans: Castle kingside to improve king safety
Coordinate pieces to support the pawn center

moveAnnotations.annotation: A powerful pin that creates immediate tactical pressure! The bishop pins the knight to the king, forcing White to deal with this threat before completing development. This move exemplifies Black's active counterplay - instead of passively defending, Black creates concrete threats that White must address. The pin also prevents White from castling kingside immediately and puts the white king in an increasingly uncomfortable position.
plans: Maintain pressure with the pin
Look for tactical opportunities arising from White's awkward king position
alternatives: 17...Be7 is more solid but less forcing

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Modern Variation is an important variation of the Alekhine's Defence. The most solid and popular line. Black develops naturally with ...Bg4, ...e6, ...Be7, castles, and then plays ...d5 to establish a firm foothold in the center. Let's walk through the key ideas.
Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the Modern Variation — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
e5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Nd5 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
d6 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
Nf3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f3 eyes important squares.
Bg4 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on g4 eyes important squares.
Be2 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on e2 eyes important squares.
e6 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
White castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority.
Be7 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on e7 eyes important squares.
c4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Nb6 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on d5 and moves to b6 where it serves the plan better.
Nc3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c3 eyes important squares.
Black castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
Be3 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on e3 eyes important squares. Notice how the pieces coordinate in this position.
d5. This is the typical position arising from the Modern Variation. From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.

The Exchange Variation is an important variation of the Alekhine's Defence. White exchanges on d6 leading to a quieter game. Black gets easy development and a comfortable position. The isolated d6 pawn is not really weak because Black has active pieces. Let's walk through the key ideas.
Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the Exchange Variation — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
e5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Nd5 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
d6 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
c4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Nb6 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on d5 and moves to b6 where it serves the plan better.
exd6 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
exd6 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Nf3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f3 eyes important squares.
Be7 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on e7 eyes important squares.
Be2 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on e2 eyes important squares.
Black castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority.
White castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority.
Nc6 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c6 eyes important squares. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
Nc3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c3 eyes important squares. Notice how the pieces coordinate in this position.
Bf5. This is the typical position arising from the Exchange Variation. From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.

The Two Pawns Attack is an important variation of the Alekhine's Defence. A more restrained version of the Four Pawns Attack. White gets a spatial advantage with c4 and d4, but Black's pieces develop actively and target the d4 pawn. Let's walk through the key ideas.
Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the Two Pawns Attack — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
e5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Nd5 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
c4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Nb6 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on d5 and moves to b6 where it serves the plan better.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
d6 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
exd6 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
exd6 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Nf3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f3 eyes important squares.
Be7 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on e7 eyes important squares.
Be2 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on e2 eyes important squares.
Black castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority.
White castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority.
Bf5 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on f5 eyes important squares. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
Nc3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c3 eyes important squares. Notice how the pieces coordinate in this position.
Nc6. This is the typical position arising from the Two Pawns Attack. From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.

The Scandinavian Variation is an important variation of the Alekhine's Defence. Black transposes into a structure with some Scandinavian flavor. The early exchange on e5 simplifies but Black's position is perfectly healthy with the fianchettoed bishop. Let's walk through the key ideas.
Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the Scandinavian Variation — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
e5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Nd5 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
d6 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
Nf3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f3 eyes important squares.
dxe5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Nxe5 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Nd7 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on d7 eyes important squares.
Nxd7 captures the knight. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Bxd7 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on d7 eyes important squares.
c4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Nb6 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on d5 and moves to b6 where it serves the plan better.
Nc3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c3 eyes important squares.
g6 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
Be2 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on e2 eyes important squares.

Bg7 — Black fianchettoes the bishop, placing it on the long diagonal where it controls the center from a distance. This is a key positional idea. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
White castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority. Notice how the pieces coordinate in this position.
O-O. This is the typical position arising from the Scandinavian Variation. From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.

In the Alekhine's Defence, the Four Pawns Attack Counter is a tactical pattern you should know. Black's pieces flood into active squares while White's overextended center buckles. The ...Bb4 pin and ...Nb4 hop create tactical threats that are very hard to meet. Let's walk through how it works.
Black plays Nf6, establishing the position. The key moment is approaching.
White plays e5, establishing the position. The key moment is approaching.
Black plays Nd5, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays d4. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with d6. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
c4 by White. The position is heading toward the critical moment.
Black plays Nb6, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays f4. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black captures with dxe5. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
White captures with fxe5. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
Black plays Nc6, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays Be3. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with Bf5. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
Nc3 by White. The position is heading toward the critical moment.
Black plays e6, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays Nf3. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with Bb4. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
Rc1 by White. The position is heading toward the critical moment.
Bxc3+. This is where the trap begins. The next two moves are the key sequence you need to memorize.
bxc3! Now the trap is revealed. White wins material with this capture.
Nb4! The trap is sprung. Black has a winning position. This is the key takeaway from the Four Pawns Attack Counter.

In the Alekhine's Defence, the Bg4 Pin Pressure is a tactical pattern you should know. After the center opens up, Black's pieces are wonderfully coordinated. The bishop on g4 pins the knight, and Black has pressure along the c-file and against the isolated d5 pawn. Let's walk through how it works.
Black plays Nf6, establishing the position. The key moment is approaching.
White plays e5, establishing the position. The key moment is approaching.
Black plays Nd5, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays d4. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with d6. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
Nf3 by White. The position is heading toward the critical moment.
Black plays Bg4, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays Be2. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with e6. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
White castles. The position looks safe, but danger lurks.
Black plays Be7, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays c4. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with Nb6. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
White captures with exd6. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
Black captures with cxd6. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
White plays d5. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
exd5. This is where the trap begins. The next two moves are the key sequence you need to memorize.
cxd5! Now the trap is revealed. White wins material with this capture.
O-O! The trap is sprung. Black has a winning position. This is the key takeaway from the Bg4 Pin Pressure.

In the Alekhine's Defence, the Four Pawns Steamroll is a dangerous line you must be aware of. If Black plays too passively against the Four Pawns Attack, White's center becomes a steamroller. The key is to challenge it immediately, not let White consolidate and build up an attack. Let's see why.
Black plays Nf6. This is the natural continuation that leads into the warning line.
White plays e5. This is the natural continuation that leads into the warning line.
Nd5. The position is sharp and requires precise play from this point forward.
White plays d4. This is the problematic continuation you need to recognize.
d6 by Black. The position is becoming uncomfortable — careful defense is needed.
White continues with c4. Watch out — a mistake here would be very costly.

In the Alekhine's Defence, the Exchange Passive Play is a dangerous line you must be aware of. In the Exchange Variation, if Black doesn't develop actively, the slightly weak d6 pawn becomes a long-term target. Always look for ...e5 or ...Bf5 to activate your pieces. Let's see why.
